text: In this video, we will describe our method for performing a robotic right hemicolectomy with central mesocolic excision. We present the case of a forty-two year old female with no significant medical or family history and no previous abdominal surgery. She underwent a colonoscopy and gastroscopy for investigation of iron deficiency anemia. A bulky lesion was found at the hepatic flexure and was determined to be a PMMR adenocarcinoma. The CEA was normal. CT imaging confirmed a large mass lesion at the hepatic flexure causing partial obstruction. No evidence of metastatic disease was found. Due to the patient's young age, the bulky and partially obstructing nature of the tumour and the presence of mesenteric adenopathy on CT, a decision was made to proceed with complete mesocolic excision. The patient was positioned supine with both arms tucked. This is our standard port configuration for a robotic right hemicolectomy. A table break was used at the hips to increase the distance between the ASIS and the lower border of the ribs. We have a gel point mini in a three centimeter fan and steel incision or one twelve millimeter port and two eight millimeter ports.

The surgeon employs a superior mesenteric vein-first technique for central mesocolic excision. The small bowel is retracted proximally using an endoractor, while the mesentery is grasped with fenestrated bipolar forceps. Monopolar curved scissors are used to dissect the peritoneum, exposing the medial aspect of the SMV to remove overlying lymphatic tissue. The iliocolic pedicle is dissected, with the iliocolic artery positioned posterior to the vein and passing under the SMB. The iliocolic vein and artery are divided together using mechanical fixation followed by an energy device or scissors, with three hemo-lock clips applied. The duodenum is dissected away and pushed posteriorly as the dissection continues along the SMV towards the pancreas, considering variations of Henle's trunk to prevent bleeding. The middle colic artery or its right branch is approached during the dissection.

The duodenum and pancreas are visible, cleared of overlying lymphatic tissue. The surgeon takes the middle colic vein, the right branch of the middle colic artery, and the right colic vein. The boundaries of the central mesocolic excision are defined, with the medial border over the top of the SMV and the superior border where the gastroepiploic vein meets the trunk of Henle. Surgery cell fibrilla is used to control venous bleeding, avoiding energy devices. The dissection of the duodenum continues using a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the ascending colon and uses a vessel sealer to divide the ileal mesentery. A SureForm 16mm blue load cartridge is employed to divide the ileum. The greater omentum is separated from the transverse colon, and the transverse colon mesentery is divided. The lesser sac is entered, and the lesser omentum is taken down. The dissection proceeds toward the hepatic flexure using a top-down approach. The colon is divided first to form the anastomosis, with a window created in the colonic mesentery. A SureForm 60mm blue load cartridge is used to divide the colon, and the mesentery is divided with a vessel sealer. The specimen is placed above the liver for retrieval at the end of the procedure.

Firefly imaging is utilized post-ICG injection to verify the vascularity of the conduits. The bowel is positioned for an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis. An enterotomy is created on the small bowel, positioned two centimeters from the staple line. The greater omentum is placed in the supracolic compartment. A colotomy is made in the tinea of the transverse colon, and a 60mm blue load Shawl Form stapler is positioned with the small jaw in the mobile small bowel and the large jaw in the large bowel. The enterotomy and colotomy are aligned evenly before firing the stapler. The common enterotomy is closed using a 3-0 V-lock suture, with sutures pulled tight at the end of each throw. A second suture is placed at the opposite end, tied together in the middle, and the anastomosis is covered with omentum.

The fan and steel incision fascia is closed with a one PDS on a CT two needle. The peritoneum is closed with a three O vicryl and the ports are closed with three O monocryl and skin glue. Postoperatively, the IDC is removed at the end of the case. Soft dye is commenced once awake. For analgesia, IV paracoxib and paracetamol and pirentopentadol are used. The patient was discharged day two post-op and had no short or long-term complications. The pathology revealed a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma extending into the deep muscularis propria. It was sixty-five millimeters in maximum dimension and the margins were clear. There was no evidence of malignancy in sixty-two lymph nodes.
